---
title: "Time Series Econometrics"
subtitle: "Dependence, trends, and dynamics over time"
---

## Overview

Cross-sectional methods assume observations are, after conditioning, independent draws. Time series breaks that assumption at its core: this month's primary-care demand, waiting times, or inflation depends on last month's. That dependence is both a nuisance (it invalidates ordinary standard errors) and an opportunity (it lets us forecast and trace dynamics).

This module builds the time-series toolkit from the ground up. It starts with stationarity and the ARIMA models that describe a single series' memory; confronts the treachery of unit roots --- where two unrelated trending series can look spuriously, significantly related --- and the cointegration that tames it; moves to vector autoregressions and impulse responses for systems of series; and ends with volatility models (ARCH/GARCH) for the time-varying variance that mean-focused models miss entirely.

## Pages in This Module

- [Stationarity and ARIMA](stationarity-arima.qmd) --- The building blocks of time-series modeling.
- [Unit Roots and Cointegration](unit-roots-cointegration.qmd) --- Random walks, spurious regression, and long-run equilibrium.
- [VAR and Impulse Responses](var-impulse-response.qmd) --- Dynamics among several series.
- [Volatility: ARCH and GARCH](volatility-garch.qmd) --- Modeling time-varying variance.
